description:
You are acting as an observability-engineer who has been asked to produce a *very tiny* benchmark that will later be imported into a
    Grafana dashboard.  Management only wants a quick “size on disk” test for a single file, captured in a reproducible CSV log.

    Perform the following steps from the Linux terminal:

    1. Create the directory
          /home/user/benchmarks
       if it does not already exist.

    2. Inside that directory create the file
          /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt
       whose **entire** contents must be exactly the single ASCII line

          The quick brown fox jumps over

       followed by a single UNIX line-feed character (hex 0x0A, “\n”).  
       There must be **no** trailing spaces after the word “over”.

    3. Measure the file’s size in bytes with the GNU core-utils program
          wc -c
       so that the command’s stdout **only** shows one line, formatted exactly like

          31 /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt

       (That is: one or more leading spaces are allowed in front of the number,
       the number “31” itself, a single space, and then the full absolute path you see above.)

    4. Save the result to the new CSV log file

          /home/user/benchmark_results.csv

       using the precise CSV schema shown here (no header row, one record only):

          command,bytes
          wc -c /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt,31

       Important formatting requirements the automated grader will check:
         • The file must be named exactly benchmark_results.csv and live directly in /home/user.
         • The file must contain **exactly** one newline-terminated line, with no trailing blank lines.
         • That line must contain exactly one comma.  
         • The left column must be the command string wc -c /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt  
           (without extra spaces in the command portion).  
         • The right column must be the integer 31 (no quotation marks, no spaces).
         • The file must end with a single trailing newline “\n”.

    5. Do not print anything other than normal command output to the terminal.  
       The final verification will only check the presence, location and
       byte-exact contents of /home/user/benchmark_results.csv and
       /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt.

    Once these steps are complete, the task is finished.
truth:
The verification script will confirm:

    1. Directory:
       • /home/user/benchmarks exists and is a directory (mode >= 755).

    2. Data file:
       • /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt exists, is a regular file,
         has size 31 bytes and its contents (as a hexdump) equal

             00000000  54 68 65 20 71 75 69 63  6b 20 62 72 6f 77 6e 20  |The quick brown |
             00000010  66 6f 78 20 6a 75 6d 70  73 20 6f 76 65 72 0a     |fox jumps over.|

    3. Log file:
       • /home/user/benchmark_results.csv exists, is a regular file,
         and its **exact** byte contents (including the terminating newline) are

             77 63 20 2d 63 20 2f 68 6f 6d 65 2f 75 73 65 72 2f 62 65 6e 63 68 6d 61 72 6b 73 2f 73 61 6d 70 6c 65 2e 74 78 74 2c 33 31 0a
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
             wc -c /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt,31\n

         which in ASCII is the single line:
             wc -c /home/user/benchmarks/sample.txt,31

         followed by exactly one “\n” (0x0A).

    4. Nothing else is required; all other files/dirs are ignored.
name: task_000000_5288ba4d
